status: CANDIDATES -- STAGED, NOT ENFORCEABLE. Static skill artifacts; no runtime analyzer is wired.
scope: Slice 1 -- SQL analytical mental model, logical query processing, grain, aggregation correctness. Slice 2 -- joins, relationship/uniqueness assumptions, duplicate (fan-out) amplification, NULL behavior in joins/filters/anti-joins. Slice 3 -- window functions (OVER/PARTITION/ORDER, frames, ranking, LAG/LEAD, where-clause restriction). Slice 4 -- date/time & time-series (truncation, time zones, date spine, half-open ranges, period-over-period, indexing). Slice 5 -- validation & reconciliation (zero-row gates, control totals, referential integrity, freshness/completeness, idempotency). Slice 6 -- performance & maintainability (sargability, SELECT *, implicit cross join, CTE grain, filter-early) + consolidation summary. | SQL Cookbook C1 -- set operations & table comparison. C2 -- DML & transformation logic. C3 -- reporting & reshaping. C4 -- string & data cleaning. C5 -- advanced date recipes. C6 -- gaps & islands + hierarchical/recursive. C7 -- metadata-driven profiling & SQL-generating-SQL.

promotion_policy.analyzer_v1: static / low false-positive: promotion target after review
promotion_policy.analyzer_v2: needs AST or schema metadata: keep staged

candidates.name: Aggregation over a join result without a stated/verified grain
candidates.rationale: Fan-out double-counting is the most dangerous silent BI-SQL bug; no error is raised.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Aggregation over a join result without a stated/verified grain
candidates.recommended_fix: Declare the result grain; verify join keys are unique on the one side; aggregate to grain before joining if fan-out is possible.

candidates.name: COUNT(column) where COUNT(*) or COUNT(DISTINCT) is likely intended
candidates.rationale: COUNT variants diverge exactly when data has nulls/duplicates, silently mis-measuring entities like orders.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: COUNT(column) where COUNT(*) or COUNT(DISTINCT) is likely intended
candidates.recommended_fix: Pick deliberately: rows -> COUNT(*); distinct entities -> COUNT(DISTINCT col); non-null values -> COUNT(col).

candidates.name: Aggregate condition placed in WHERE (or row condition in HAVING)
candidates.rationale: Reflects a misunderstanding of logical query processing order; query errors or wrong intent.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Aggregate condition placed in WHERE (or row condition in HAVING)
candidates.recommended_fix: Filter groups/aggregates in HAVING; keep row-level conditions in WHERE.

candidates.name: Join on a column not asserted unique on the one side
candidates.rationale: Unverified uniqueness is the root cause of fan-out and broken reconciliation.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Join on a column not asserted unique on the one side
candidates.recommended_fix: Verify uniqueness of the join key on the one side (GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*)>1); dedup or change grain if not unique.

candidates.name: Aggregate or denominator sensitive to NULLs without explicit handling
candidates.rationale: Nulls silently change counts, averages, and filters; for validation this must be intentional.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Aggregate or denominator sensitive to NULLs without explicit handling
candidates.recommended_fix: Decide null treatment explicitly (COALESCE to a default, or document exclusion); beware NOT IN with nullable subqueries.

candidates.name: AVG of pre-aggregated values (average of averages)
candidates.rationale: Unweighted averages of averages ignore group sizes and give a wrong overall figure.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: AVG of pre-aggregated values (average of averages)
candidates.recommended_fix: Average from the base grain (SUM(x)/SUM(n)) or weight by group size; don't average group averages unweighted.

candidates.name: DISTINCT used to suppress duplicates without explaining grain
candidates.rationale: DISTINCT masks fan-out created earlier and can still leave additive aggregates wrong.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: DISTINCT used to suppress duplicates without explaining grain
candidates.recommended_fix: Identify why duplicates exist (which join fanned out), fix the grain; use DISTINCT only when distinctness is the genuine intent, with grain stated.

candidates.name: SUM/COUNT after a join whose right-side key is not verified unique
candidates.rationale: Fan-out silently multiplies additive aggregates; the #1 cause of inflated BI totals.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: SUM/COUNT after a join whose right-side key is not verified unique
candidates.recommended_fix: Verify right-side join-key uniqueness; aggregate to grain before joining or join to a one-row-per-key summary. Compare COUNT(*) before/after.

candidates.name: Many-to-many join without a bridge or pre-aggregation
candidates.rationale: Combinatorial row multiplication corrupts grain and every additive metric.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Many-to-many join without a bridge or pre-aggregation
candidates.recommended_fix: Pre-aggregate one side to one row per key or use a bridge; never aggregate directly across an unresolved many-to-many.

candidates.name: NOT IN against a subquery/column that may contain NULL
candidates.rationale: A single NULL makes NOT IN return zero rows silently (three-valued logic).
candidates.example_signal: Signal: NOT IN against a subquery/column that may contain NULL
candidates.recommended_fix: Use NOT EXISTS or LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL; if NOT IN is required, exclude nulls explicitly.

candidates.name: Right-table predicate of a LEFT JOIN placed in WHERE
candidates.rationale: It silently converts a LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN, dropping rows and understating totals.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Right-table predicate of a LEFT JOIN placed in WHERE
candidates.recommended_fix: Move the right-table condition into the ON clause to preserve unmatched left rows.

candidates.name: Deduplication without a deterministic survivor
candidates.rationale: DISTINCT/GROUP BY dedup is non-deterministic about which row wins; silver/gold dedup must be explainable.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Deduplication without a deterministic survivor
candidates.recommended_fix: Dedup with ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY key ORDER BY <tiebreak>) and filter rn=1; document the survivor rule.

candidates.name: Ordered window function without a deterministic ORDER BY tiebreak
candidates.rationale: Ties produce nondeterministic ROW_NUMBER/rank and dedup survivors that change between runs.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Ordered window function without a deterministic ORDER BY tiebreak
candidates.recommended_fix: Add a tiebreak column to the OVER ORDER BY so the ordering is total (e.g. a unique key).

candidates.name: Running aggregate with ORDER BY but no explicit ROWS frame
candidates.rationale: The default RANGE frame makes running totals jump at duplicate ORDER BY values.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Running aggregate with ORDER BY but no explicit ROWS frame
candidates.recommended_fix: Specify ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for row-by-row accumulation; the RANGE default groups tied rows.

candidates.name: LAST_VALUE/FIRST_VALUE without an explicit frame
candidates.rationale: The default frame ends at the current row, so LAST_VALUE returns the current row, not the partition's last.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: LAST_VALUE/FIRST_VALUE without an explicit frame
candidates.recommended_fix: Add ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING to read the true partition first/last.

candidates.name: Window function referenced in WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING
candidates.rationale: Window functions are computed at SELECT (after WHERE/HAVING); using them earlier is invalid.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Window function referenced in WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING
candidates.recommended_fix: Compute the window in a subquery/CTE and filter it in an outer query.

candidates.name: LAG/LEAD for period-over-period without a date spine
candidates.rationale: LAG reads the previous row, not the previous calendar period; gaps silently break MoM/YoY.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: LAG/LEAD for period-over-period without a date spine
candidates.recommended_fix: Join to a complete date spine so periods are contiguous before applying LAG/LEAD.

candidates.name: BETWEEN on a timestamp/date column where a half-open range is intended
candidates.rationale: BETWEEN is inclusive of the end instant and silently undercounts the last day.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: BETWEEN on a timestamp/date column where a half-open range is intended
candidates.recommended_fix: Use >= start AND < next_start; avoid BETWEEN on timestamps (drops the end day's time-of-day).

candidates.name: Grouping by a raw timestamp instead of truncating to a grain
candidates.rationale: Near-unique timestamps produce one group per instant; no real aggregation.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Grouping by a raw timestamp instead of truncating to a grain
candidates.recommended_fix: Truncate to the reporting grain (DATE_TRUNC) or group by a date-dimension period column.

candidates.name: Date bucketing without explicit time-zone handling
candidates.rationale: Zone mismatches shift events across midnight and corrupt daily counts and reconciliation.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Date bucketing without explicit time-zone handling
candidates.recommended_fix: Store UTC; convert to the business/local zone before truncating or bucketing to a day/period.

candidates.name: Period-over-period or gap-sensitive series without a date spine
candidates.rationale: Sparse periods make 'previous row' != 'previous period', silently breaking YoY/MoM and rolling windows.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Period-over-period or gap-sensitive series without a date spine
candidates.recommended_fix: LEFT JOIN a complete date spine (calendar table) so periods are contiguous before LAG/LEAD/rolling.

candidates.name: Function wrapped on a date column in WHERE (non-sargable)
candidates.rationale: Wrapping the column prevents index use and is error-prone at boundaries.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Function wrapped on a date column in WHERE (non-sargable)
candidates.recommended_fix: Filter the bare column with a range predicate (>= start AND < next_start) instead of YEAR()/DATE_TRUNC() in WHERE.

candidates.name: Comparing a partial period to a full period
candidates.rationale: Unequal period lengths make period-over-period comparisons misleading.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Comparing a partial period to a full period
candidates.recommended_fix: Compare like-for-like period lengths (e.g. MTD vs same-MTD last period) or explicitly label the partial comparison.

candidates.name: Transformation/promotion path with no validation gate
candidates.rationale: Unvalidated tables propagate duplicates, nulls, and orphans into measures and dashboards.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Transformation/promotion path with no validation gate
candidates.recommended_fix: Add zero-row gates for uniqueness, not-null, and referential integrity before promoting to the next layer.

candidates.name: Layer-to-layer data movement without a control-total reconciliation
candidates.rationale: Without a control total, dropped or inflated data passes silently.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Layer-to-layer data movement without a control-total reconciliation
candidates.recommended_fix: Reconcile an invariant value total (revenue/quantity) plus distinct-entity counts across source/silver/gold.

candidates.name: Reconciliation comparing mismatched grains
candidates.rationale: Grain mismatch creates false mismatches or hides real ones.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Reconciliation comparing mismatched grains
candidates.recommended_fix: Align both sides to a shared grain before comparing totals.

candidates.name: Reconciliation on row counts only (no value tie-out)
candidates.rationale: Counts can match while sums drift (fan-out) or values change with counts unchanged.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Reconciliation on row counts only (no value tie-out)
candidates.recommended_fix: Pair row counts with a value control total and distinct-entity counts.

candidates.name: Periodic load without a freshness/completeness gate
candidates.rationale: Correct-but-stale or partially-loaded data passes other gates undetected.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Periodic load without a freshness/completeness gate
candidates.recommended_fix: Add a recency check (max date within SLA) and a spine-based completeness check for missing periods/segments.

candidates.name: SELECT * in a persisted transformation or view
candidates.rationale: Reads/stores unneeded columns, breaks on schema drift, and hides the step's grain/contract.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: SELECT * in a persisted transformation or view
candidates.recommended_fix: List explicit output columns; declare the step's grain. Reserve SELECT * for ad-hoc exploration.

candidates.name: Join with no ON condition (implicit cross join)
candidates.rationale: A Cartesian product explodes rows and corrupts every aggregate.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Join with no ON condition (implicit cross join)
candidates.recommended_fix: Add an explicit, correct ON condition; if a cross join is intended, write CROSS JOIN deliberately.

candidates.name: Non-sargable predicate (function-wrapped column or leading-wildcard LIKE)
candidates.rationale: Prevents index/zone-map use, forcing full scans.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Non-sargable predicate (function-wrapped column or leading-wildcard LIKE)
candidates.recommended_fix: Keep the column bare; use range predicates; avoid LIKE '%x'.

candidates.name: CTE stack without declared grain per step
candidates.rationale: Fan-out and double-counting hide in untracked CTE grain; review becomes unreliable.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: CTE stack without declared grain per step
candidates.recommended_fix: Comment each CTE with its grain; make grain transitions explicit.

candidates.name: Selective filter applied after an expensive join/aggregate
candidates.rationale: Processing far more rows than necessary; slow and memory-heavy.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Selective filter applied after an expensive join/aggregate
candidates.recommended_fix: Push the most selective filters before joins/aggregations so fewer rows flow downstream.

candidates.name: UNION used where UNION ALL is likely intended
candidates.rationale: Dropping legitimate duplicate rows understates counts/totals in BI.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: UNION used where UNION ALL is likely intended
candidates.recommended_fix: Use UNION ALL unless duplicate removal is intended; UNION silently dedupes and adds sort cost.

candidates.name: Set operation with mismatched/misordered column projections
candidates.rationale: Set ops compare/stack by position, not name -- wrong columns silently combined.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Set operation with mismatched/misordered column projections
candidates.recommended_fix: Project identical columns, same order, compatible types on both sides; set ops match by position.

candidates.name: UPDATE/DELETE without a WHERE clause
candidates.rationale: A missing WHERE mutates every row -- large-scale silent corruption.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: UPDATE/DELETE without a WHERE clause
candidates.recommended_fix: Scope every UPDATE/DELETE with a precise WHERE; preview with a SELECT first.

candidates.name: Append INSERT load where MERGE/replace (idempotent) is needed
candidates.rationale: Append loads double rows/totals on re-run and break reconciliation.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Append INSERT load where MERGE/replace (idempotent) is needed
candidates.recommended_fix: Load layers with MERGE/upsert or replace keyed on a unique business key so reloads are idempotent.

candidates.name: Update-from / MERGE source matches multiple rows per target key
candidates.rationale: Multi-match makes the update nondeterministic or errors.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Update-from / MERGE source matches multiple rows per target key
candidates.recommended_fix: Ensure the source is unique per target key (verify) or pre-aggregate before the update/merge.

candidates.name: UNION of separate GROUP BY aggregations instead of ROLLUP/GROUPING SETS
candidates.rationale: Hand-unioned aggregations are slow, verbose, and desync between levels.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: UNION of separate GROUP BY aggregations instead of ROLLUP/GROUPING SETS
candidates.recommended_fix: Compute multiple subtotal levels with one ROLLUP/CUBE/GROUPING SETS query.

candidates.name: ROLLUP/CUBE result without GROUPING() to flag subtotal rows
candidates.rationale: Subtotal NULLs are read as real categories or double-counted downstream.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: ROLLUP/CUBE result without GROUPING() to flag subtotal rows
candidates.recommended_fix: Use GROUPING(col) to distinguish subtotal/total rows from real NULL categories; filter subtotals before re-aggregating.

candidates.name: Join/GROUP BY on a raw text column without normalization
candidates.rationale: Case/whitespace variants split groups and miss matches -- silent count/reconciliation errors.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Join/GROUP BY on a raw text column without normalization
candidates.recommended_fix: Canonicalize text keys (TRIM/UPPER/unaccent) before grouping/joining, or key on a surrogate.

candidates.name: CAST of text to number/date without safe parsing
candidates.rationale: One garbage value errors the load or silently mis-converts.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: CAST of text to number/date without safe parsing
candidates.recommended_fix: Use TRY_CAST or a pattern guard; quarantine unparseable values instead of hard-casting.

candidates.name: Hardcoded date list instead of a generated calendar spine
candidates.rationale: Hardcoded/implicit dates are brittle and let missing periods vanish.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Hardcoded date list instead of a generated calendar spine
candidates.recommended_fix: Generate a contiguous calendar (recursive CTE / numbers table) and LEFT JOIN facts to it.

candidates.name: Non-canonical interval-overlap boundary logic
candidates.rationale: Wrong boundary logic double-counts touching intervals or misses containment.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Non-canonical interval-overlap boundary logic
candidates.recommended_fix: Use the canonical half-open overlap test: a_start < b_end AND b_start < a_end.

candidates.name: Recursive CTE without a termination/cycle/depth guard
candidates.rationale: Missing guards cause infinite recursion on deep or cyclic data.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Recursive CTE without a termination/cycle/depth guard
candidates.recommended_fix: Ensure the recursive member terminates; add a path-based cycle guard and a depth cap.

candidates.name: Hardcoded table/column list instead of catalog-driven checks
candidates.rationale: Hand-listed schemas go stale; drift silently breaks checks or leaves blind spots.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Hardcoded table/column list instead of catalog-driven checks
candidates.recommended_fix: Generate checks/profiles from information_schema; reconcile against expected schema to detect drift.

candidates.name: Generated SQL concatenates identifiers/literals without quoting
candidates.rationale: Unquoted concatenation produces broken or injectable SQL.
candidates.example_signal: Signal: Generated SQL concatenates identifiers/literals without quoting
candidates.recommended_fix: Quote identifiers (quote_ident) and escape literals (quote_literal) when generating SQL from metadata.

promotion_readiness.note: Consolidated view (Slice 6). analyzer_v1 = static/low-FP, promotion targets after review; analyzer_v2 = needs AST/metadata, stay staged; human_guidance_only = judgment, never auto-enforced.

promotion_log: 2026-06-26 draft: 10 analyzer_v1 candidates promoted into sql-analyzer-rules.json (see promoted_to). Remaining staged: analyzer_v2 + human_guidance_only.
